The layout of the buttons and paddles is usually standardised, although Williams made a totally custom wheel for Kubica, and Hamilton always used a different clutch padel setup at Mercedes compared to Bottas, so customisation is possible. What the buttons actually do can also be configured for driver preference, and you often see this when comparing images of teammates steering wheels. It's not uncommon to have two different button layouts depending on driver preference.

The issue wasn't necessarily sealing the floor itself (it was actually quite good at that, even too good) but more that Mercedes assumed they could control their rear ride heights a lot better than they actually could.

Considered but crucially, ultimately dumped. That whole idea fell apart pretty brutally hard (not just the zeropods, admittedly) once the rubber met the road. Of course a Venturi car is going to be REALLY fast if you've got an absolute fuckload of floor surface and you run it extremely close to the track. The problem is rear ride height control and Mercedes could never figure that out. I don't think there was any figuring that out without straight up active suspension, though.

I think that was also partially a hang over from their dominant phase. There was a series of articles on the BBC by someone called secret F1 aerodynamicst, widely believed to be James Allison, who talked about how Mercedes always went against the grain in the old regulation set pre ground effect which obviously worked very well for them then. The primary example was Mercedes’ low rake philosophy and how the entire grid pursued the Newey lead high rake philosophy. I think Mercedes was just hellbent on sticking to that kind of philosophy in the ground effect era too hoping it would magically click

how does every team that gets in bed with honda hit the exact same problems every time and everyone involved is like "how could we have known?" It's baffling to me, Honda have a very odd attitude to this projects and teams keep asking honda to hit these shorter development cycles than everyone else for no reason i can fathom. AM had merc engines, they could have signed a deal for another year kept the honda deal quiet and just changed a year later. But they should know who was going to be working for honda, the exact size of the workforce, how much they were going to invest all before signing a deal. they should have a permanent team who can speak japanese over there working with them and constant communication between the teams. Honda/japanese work culture issues and the way to mitigate them has somehow been a problem and then has to be fixed the exact same way each time yet each new deal faces the same issues over and over again. Even last time, honda themselves didn't exactly create a good engine, they had to replace the boss then they had to bring in ilmor to fix half their problems and make the engine not shite.

I highly doubt he's doing a multivariate linear mixed effects model for this... Even if he is, the LMM would have to rely quite a bit on partial pooling which would shrink the estimates quite severely since the data is pretty limited. I'm not even going to get into the assumptions of this model including normality, constant variance (with a Frequentist framework even modelling sigma is hard and pretty much impossible with this amount of data, etc. It's a meme analysis but I always get concerned since a ton of poeple may not understand that.

This incident had nothing to do with the brakes. His left front tire was covered in asphalt marbles from driving over the broken up section on the outer left side of the corner, just like Stroll. Drivers barely even use the brakes into the final corner, they use them much more into the second to last corner after the swimming pool section. He avoided that part on all the safety car laps before, but as it was ending he fucked up and drove directly over it. It was 100% his fault.
